metadata.seed: Well, I have to buy everything in pairs or in twos. And if I only have one thing, I won't buy it. I'll put it back or I'll look around the store for another thing so that I have two things to buy.

patient.query: Well, I have to buy everything in pairs or in twos. And if I only have one thing, I won't buy it. I'll put it back or I'll look around the store for another thing so that I have two things to buy.

automatic_thoughts: I need to buy in pairs
One item is not enough

neutral: Tends to be preoccupied with order (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you feel a strong need to have items in pairs when you shop. What evidence do you have that buying a single item would be a problem? Have you ever bought just one item and felt okay, or have you noticed any difference in how you feel?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Tends to be preoccupied with order (finding)
retrieved_concepts_used.label: neutral

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I do tend to buy things in pairs. It just... feels right, like I need to balance things out. If I only have one, I start to worry that it’ll be lonely or something. I’d probably feel unsettled or anxious, like I’m missing something. It's hard to explain, but it just creates this nagging feeling in my chest if I don’t have that extra item.

automatic_thoughts: I need to balance things out
the item will be lonely
I'm missing something

neutral: Obsessional thoughts of throwing things away (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you feel a strong urge to keep things balanced, and that missing a pair makes you anxious. You might be assuming that one item will always feel lonely. What evidence do you have that this is always true, and are there times when a single item has felt fine?

patient.query: I guess I just remember times when I had something on its own, and I felt... off. Like when I had just one shoe, it just felt wrong. But it’s not like I’ve really tested it much. I avoid it, because the thought of being without that balance makes me uneasy. It’s easier to just stick with what I know, even if it feels a bit silly sometimes.

automatic_thoughts: it feels wrong
the thought of being without balance makes me uneasy
it's easier to stick with what I know

neutral: Able to adapt to sensory changes in body part (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you feel unsettled when things aren’t balanced. What does that unsettled feeling look like for you? Have you ever tried a situation where you had an imbalance and what happened?

patient.query: Um, it’s like this tightness in my chest, maybe a bit of nausea sometimes. I just kind of spiral into thinking that something bad might happen if I don’t have that balance. I did try once, I think, to just have one of something... and I ended up feeling really anxious, like I was waiting for something to go wrong. It’s like I almost convinced myself that if I had the extra, it would keep everything in check.

triggers: trying to have one of something
automatic_thoughts: something bad might happen if I don’t have that balance
if I had the extra, it would keep everything in check
waiting for something to go wrong

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling a lot of pressure to keep everything in a certain state. What evidence do you have that something bad will happen if you don’t maintain that balance? Have you ever experienced a time when you didn’t have that exact balance and things still worked out? How might you view the situation if you considered other possibilities?

patient.query: I mean, I can’t really pinpoint any specific evidence, you know? It’s more like this nagging fear that just sticks with me. I guess I have had times when I didn’t have that balance, but I just focused on the worry instead of what actually happened. It’s like I’m afraid to let my guard down, because I think if I think differently, then I’m opening myself up to something bad happening. It’s just... safer to stick to what I believe.

automatic_thoughts: I can't pinpoint any specific evidence
It's safer to stick to what I believe
If I think differently, something bad will happen
behaviors: afraid to let my guard down

entailment: Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like this fear has been a constant companion, making it hard to let your guard down. You seem to feel that any shift in thinking could open the door to something bad, which can feel very protective but also exhausting. It’s understandable that you’d want to stay in the safety of what you know.

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: fear of saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: entailment

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: belief that changing thoughts leads to danger
retrieved_concepts_used.label: unknown

patient.query: Yeah, it does feel exhausting. I just... I don’t want to be caught off guard or feel vulnerable. It’s like I’m always waiting for the other shoe to drop, you know? But at the same time, I see how it limits me. It’s frustrating because I want to feel free but then I get trapped in these cycles of fear. Sometimes I wonder if I’m missing out on something better by holding on so tightly.

triggers: waiting for the other shoe to drop
automatic_thoughts: I don’t want to be caught off guard
I see how it limits me
I want to feel free
I wonder if I’m missing out

entailment: Fear of being left alone during period of dependence (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing a pattern where feeling vulnerable feels like a risk. What moments have you had where being open didn’t lead to being caught off guard? How might you view those moments differently?

patient.query: Hmm, there have been a few times where I’ve tried being open, and honestly, it didn’t turn out as bad as I thought it would. Like, I remember once sharing a personal story with a friend, and they really listened and supported me instead of judging me. But I just brushed it off later, like it was a fluke or something. I guess I still tend to focus on the times it didn’t go well, and it feels like that’s the safer narrative to hold

triggers: sharing a personal story with a friend
automatic_thoughts: it didn’t turn out as bad as I thought it would
it was a fluke
focus on the times it didn’t go well
that’s the safer narrative to hold

neutral: Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)
Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you had a supportive moment, yet you still feel it was just a fluke. What evidence led you to that conclusion? How might you view that experience if you considered it a meaningful moment rather than an exception?

patient.query: I guess the evidence I lean on is all the times I’ve opened up and it hasn’t gone well. Those moments stick with me, you know? So when something good happens, I just push it aside, thinking it’s not how things usually go for me. But if I were to see it differently, maybe I could think that not every interaction has to follow that pattern. It just feels really vulnerable to let that in, because then I’d have to face the idea that things could really

automatic_thoughts: it hasn’t gone well
not how things usually go for me
not every interaction has to follow that pattern

neutral: Character trait finding of ability to learn from adverse experience (finding)
Able to recognize own emotional pain and distress (finding)
Tends to fail to learn from adverse experience (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear how past experiences of opening up have left you feeling uneasy, and it’s understandable that you might want to protect yourself by setting aside positive moments. Yet, if you look at the times when sharing did not go poorly, what might those moments reveal? It could be helpful to consider that each interaction is unique, and that the pattern you fear may not be inevitable.

patient.query: Yeah, I get what you’re saying. It makes sense on some level, but it’s like, my mind just keeps going back to the negatives. Those moments when things went well feel like a small exception to the rule, not the rule itself. I mean, what if the next time I open up, it goes badly again? It’s just hard to shift that fear. I know there's a part of me that wants to believe what you're saying, but another part just... clings to

automatic_thoughts: my mind just keeps going back to the negatives
those moments when things went well feel like a small exception to the rule
what if the next time I open up, it goes badly again?

neutral: Loss of hope for the future (finding)
Enduring personality change after catastrophic experience (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing a pattern where the negative thoughts feel more real than the positive ones. What evidence do you have that the next time you open up will go badly? Have there been times when it went well, and what was different then? How might you view those successes as part of the bigger picture rather than just exceptions?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess the negative thoughts do feel more real, like they’re just louder somehow. I can’t really think of anything specific that guarantees it’ll go badly next time; it’s more like... a fear that’s built up from past experiences. Those times when it went well, I think maybe it was just the person I was with, or I was in a good headspace. But it still feels like those moments are rare, you know? Even if they happened, it’s like

automatic_thoughts: negative thoughts feel more real
fear built up from past experiences
those moments are rare

llm_response.response: It sounds like those negative thoughts feel especially vivid and you’re unsure if they’ll predict a bad outcome. What evidence do you have that these thoughts actually come true, and what evidence might suggest otherwise? How might you view those rare positive moments differently—could they offer clues about what conditions help you feel safe?
